facts: Washington Union Station opened in 1907 and was the largest train station in the world at the time.
During World War II, Union Station handled over 200,000 passengers per day at its wartime peak.
The station's main hall ceiling soars 96 feet high, modeled after the ancient Baths of Diocletian in Rome.
After decades of decline, Union Station was fully restored in a $160 million renovation completed in 1988.
VRE was founded in 1992 and today carries around 19,000 daily commuters between Northern Virginia and DC.
MARC Train covers 178 route miles across three lines — Penn, Camden, and Brunswick.
Amtrak's Acela, which stops here, is the fastest train in the Americas, reaching up to 150 mph.
Union Station is the second busiest rail station in the US, with over 5 million Amtrak passengers per year.
Union Station was designed by Daniel Burnham, the same architect behind Chicago's famous 1893 World's Fair buildings.
The station was built on a swamp near the U.S. Capitol, requiring significant land reclamation before construction could begin.
Union Station is the southern terminus of the Northeast Corridor, the busiest passenger rail line in the nation.
The station carries the IATA airport code ZWU, treating it as an official transport hub on par with major airports.
In 1976, a failed attempt to convert Union Station into a National Visitor Center left the building in disrepair for years.
Union Station's lowest point came in 1981 when a leaky roof caused pieces of the ceiling to collapse into the main waiting room.
Amtrak took over full control of Union Station in 2024 and is currently planning a major renovation and expansion.
Before Union Station opened, DC's railroads operated out of two separate competing stations — one for the B&O and one for the Pennsylvania Railroad.
The Metro's Union Station stop is the busiest in the entire WMATA system, averaging over 12,000 tapped entries per day in 2024.
A 600-foot pedestrian tunnel was built during original Metro construction beneath the station but was boarded up before opening and is now used for equipment storage.
MARC's Penn Line is the fastest commuter rail line in North America, with trains capable of running up to 125 mph on the Northeast Corridor.
MARC commuter service traces its roots to 1974, but wasn't officially branded 'MARC' until after a 1984 marketing study.
At Union Station, southbound Amtrak trains swap electric locomotives for diesel engines because the rail tunnel into Virginia isn't electrified.
The station hosts over 40 million total visitors per year — far more than just rail passengers — making it one of the most visited destinations in DC.
Amtrak's Acela can cover the 225 miles between Washington and New York City in under three hours.
MARC's Brunswick Line is the only MARC line where all platforms are low-level, a legacy of its origins on the old B&O Railroad right-of-way.
The USO operated a canteen inside Union Station throughout World War II to serve the hundreds of thousands of troops passing through daily.
